message.reasoning_content:
We need to produce a complete list of candidates 1-8 (HVS1 through HVS10) as per roster, but note roster only gave stubs for 001-008 (HVS1,4,5,6,7,8,9,10). Actually the scaffold says "candidates_found" with 22 candidates, but the stage 2 roster only lists first 8. The task: "Fill THESE CANDIDATES" from the provided roster stubs. So we need to fill only those 8 candidates, and return a JSON with "candidates" array containing the full CandidateRecord objects for those 8. No other candidates. We must use the scaffold method_chain (existing step ids) for method_refs. We need to extract quantities from the ECSV tables and LaTeX sources. Let's go step by step for each candidate.

We need to construct the full CandidateRecord JSON for each. They are: HVS1, HVS4, HVS5, HVS6, HVS7, HVS8, HVS9, HVS10.

First, identifiers: we already have paper_candidate_id, all array with SDSS name. We need gaia_source_id: the paper does not provide explicit Gaia source IDs. So set to "".

Inclusion assessment: For each, they are HVS stars, originally unbound. The paper includes them in the HVS candidate list, considers them possibly unbound. So paper_labels: ["hvs_candidate"] and maybe also "unbound_star"? Paper labels include "HVS" and "unbound". We'll check: The paper classifies them as HVS, which were originally considered unbound. In the text, they are part of the "sample of HVSs" and they get bound probabilities. We'll set galactic_bound_claim: "possibly_unbound" maybe? Actually the scaffold set candidate_groups_considered for "mmt-unbound-stars". We'll need to determine for each star whether the paper claims they are unbound, likely unbound, etc. The bound probability P_b is given in the kinematic tables. For HVS1, P_b = 0% in both models, so "unbound". For HVS4, P_b = 0%, "unbound". HVS5: P_b = 0%, "unbound". HVS6: P_b = 0%, "unbound". HVS7: P_b = 7% (Model I) and 0% (Model II) - still likely unbound? But paper says "bound probability less than 50%" for most unconstrained stars. For HVS7, P_b = 7%, which is >5% but still low. The paper does not reclassify them as bound. So galactic_bound_claim could be "likely_unbound" if P_b small. But the paper doesn't definitively state unbound; they say "most objects in this group are likely unbound". HVS7 falls in "outer rim origin" group where they say "most objects in this group are likely unbound." So I'll set "likely_unbound" for those with P_b < 50% and not 100%. But for HVS7 P_b 7% (Model I) and 0% (Model II). So likely unbound. For HVS8: P_b 18% (I), 0% (II). In outer rim group, likely unbound. HVS9: P_b 0% both, unbound. HVS10: P_b 1% (I), 0% (II), unbound. So for HVS1,4,5,6,9,10 set "unbound"; for HVS7,8 set "likely_unbound". Also note HVS1 is from unconstrained group but bound probability 0%, so unbound.

Inclusion basis: "explicit_candidate_text" because the paper explicitly labels them as HVS candidates and includes them in kinematic analysis of HVSs. Extraction confidence: high, because the paper clearly identifies them as HVS and provides kinematic assessment.

Candidate origin: For these stars, they were previously identified as HVS by the MMT survey (Brown et al. 2014 and earlier). This paper is Paper III in a series, so they are not introduced by this paper; they are cited from literature. So origin_type: "cited_from_literature". paper_reassesses_unbound_status: true because this paper recalculates bound probabilities and kinematic analysis. citation: need bibkey, authors, year, title, etc. We need to identify the original discovery paper. For the HVS stars, the MMT survey discovery: Brown et al. 2014 (2014ApJ...787...89B) and earlier. The paper references Brown et al. 2014ApJ...787...89B for the sample. Let's check the bibliography: In aa.bbl, there is a reference: \bibitem[{{Brown} {et~al.}(2014){Brown}, {Geller}, \& {Kenyon}}]{2014ApJ...787...89B} with authors Brown, Geller, Kenyon, 2014, ApJ, 787, 89. That's probably the survey. The HVS stars were discovered in that survey. We'll use that as citation. The paper also mentions some HVS were discovered earlier (e.g., HVS1 from 2005), but the survey compilation is Brown+2014. For consistency, we'll use that. Citation_context_refs: need to find where the paper cites that as the source of the sample. In aa.tex line 83: "the MMT HVS survey \citep{2009ApJ...690.1639B,2014ApJ...787...89B}." And line 97: "the MMT HVS sample \citep{2018ApJ...866...39B}". The sample of 42 stars is from Brown et al. 2014. So we'll cite the 2014ApJ...787...89B bibitem. We'll use bibkey "2014ApJ...787...89B". We'll extract citation details from the bbl entry. Authors: Brown, W.~R., Geller, M.~J., & Kenyon, S.~J. Year 2014. Title not in bbl, but we can get from the context: the paper is about the MMT HVS survey. The bbl entry: {Brown}, W.~R., {Geller}, M.~J., \& {Kenyon}, S.~J. 2014, \apj, 787, 89. We don't have full title. The skill says "Only fill title, year, authors, DOI, bibcode, or arXiv ID when that field is supported by the cited bibliography refs." The bbl entry doesn't include title, DOI, etc. So we can only fill authors, year, bibcode. We'll set doi empty, title empty, arxiv_id empty. Citation_context_refs: find where the paper mentions the MMT HVS sample as source. Line 82-83: "The first HVS survey ... later extended using the MMT ... the so-called MMT HVS survey \citep{2009ApJ...690.1639B,2014ApJ...787...89B}." And line 97: "this work ... extend ... to 40 out of the 42 high-velocity outliers of the MMT HVS sample". Also line 459: "sample of HVSs of \citet{2014ApJ...787...89B}". So we can use a text ref covering those lines. We'll pick a specific one: line 459? Actually line 459: "the sample of HVSs of \citet{2014ApJ...787...89B}" (appendix). Let's extract from aa.tex line 459: "We carried out an extensive analysis of the sample of HVSs of \citet{2014ApJ...787...89B} using their spectra". So that's a clear citation of the source. We'll use start_line=459, end_line=459, context="sample of HVSs of Brown et al. 2014". That's the citation. For bibliography_refs: we need to point to the entry in aa.bbl. The bbl line for that bibitem is from line 80 to 83? Let's check aa.bbl: lines 80-83: "\bibitem[{{Brown} {et~al.}(2014){Brown}, {Geller}, \& {Kenyon}}]{2014ApJ...787...89B} ..." So can use text ref with path "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.bbl", start_line=80, end_line=83, context="bibitem 2014ApJ...787...89B". We'll include that.

Now core quantities: observed_phase_space: ra, dec, distance, parallax, proper_motion_ra, proper_motion_dec, radial_velocity. The paper gives kinematic tables that include positions and velocities, but those are Galactocentric, not celestial. The observed phase space RA/Dec and proper motions are not directly in the tables; they come from Gaia DR2 and are used to compute the kinematics. The paper does not list RA/Dec in tables; however, the SDSS names give approximate coordinates. But we need to extract the actual values used. The paper states positions and proper motions taken from Gaia DR2 and HST. But they are not explicitly tabulated. The paper's kinematic tables start from Galactocentric coordinates, not celestial. So we cannot directly fill RA/Dec from tables. We need to check if any table gives celestial coordinates. The tables "atmos-results" and "stellar_params" do not include RA/Dec. The kinematic tables give x,y,z (Galactocentric). There is no direct RA/Dec column. The paper might mention that they used positions from Gaia DR2 but does not list them. So we may need to leave ra and dec as null or unknown? The schema allows null, but we must fill if available. The paper says they complemented positions and proper motions from Gaia DR2 with radial velocities and distances. The positions are likely from Gaia DR2, but not listed. So we cannot provide a raw_value. We'll have to set ra and dec to null. However, we can infer from SDSS names: SDSS J090744.99+024506.8 gives ra=09h07m44.99s, dec=+02d45m06.8s, but that's not directly provided by the paper as a measured quantity used in the kinematic analysis; they used Gaia DR2 positions. We cannot assume the SDSS name coordinates are the exact Gaia positions used. So safer to leave null. But the schema requires at least the fields present? For ObservedPhaseSpace, ra and dec are optional (anyOf null). So we can set null. Similarly, proper motions: they are not given directly; the kinematic tables give derived velocities. The paper says they used proper motions from Gaia DR2 and HST, but not listed. So we can't fill proper_motion_ra and proper_motion_dec. Parallax: not used because uncertain; so null. Distance: provided in stellar_params table as d (kpc). For HVS1, distance = 161^{+13}_{-13} kpc from table_stellar_params.ecsv line 39. So we can fill distance. Radial velocity: from atmospheric_params_1.tex line 9: HVS1 $832.5$ km/s. So we can fill radial_velocity.

So observed_phase_space will have distance and radial_velocity.

Derived kinematics: The scaffold step-08 produces Galactocentric positions and velocities, total velocity, rest-frame velocity. We can fill from kinematic tables AS (Model I) or TF? We can choose Model I as default (the paper primarily uses Model I). The schema allows multiple values? Only one galactocentric_x etc. per candidate. We'll use Model I values. The kinematic table AS.tex gives x,y,z,vx,vy,vz, v_Grf. For each star, we'll fill galactocentric_x, y, z, vx, vy, vz, and total_velocity (v_Grf) and perhaps galactocentric_tangential_velocity? That is not given separately. So we'll fill all from AS table. For HVS1: x=-101.4, y=-100.9, z=83.5, vx=-390, vy=-340, vz=450, v_Grf=710. Also note: the table also gives escape velocity excess and P_b. So derived kinematics.

Bound assessment: escape_velocity? Not directly given; we have v_Grf - v_esc = 400 km/s, so v_esc = v_Grf - 400 = 710 - 400 = 310? But that's not an absolute value, just difference. We could fill escape_velocity from that difference, but need raw_value. The table gives "\varv_{\mathrm{Grf}}-\varv_{\mathrm{esc}}" column. We can compute escape_velocity as v_Grf - (v_Grf - v_esc) if we assume the paper's definition. But careful: the subtraction might not be exactly that. Actually v_Grf - v_esc is the excess, so escape velocity = v_Grf - (excess). That gives 710 - 400 = 310 km/s. But need to confirm if that's indeed the local escape velocity at the star's position. The paper defines v_Grf as Galactic rest-frame velocity, and v_esc is the local escape velocity. So yes. We'll compute escape_velocity = v_Grf - (v_Grf - v_esc). But we need raw_value; we don't have a direct raw value for escape velocity. The paper might not give escape velocity explicitly. The column is the difference. So we can set escape_velocity as null if not directly reported. Alternatively, we could fill with the computed value but without raw_value, that's not allowed. So better to leave null. escape_velocity_ratio: maybe we can compute v_Grf / v_esc but same issue. So we'll set null. bound_probability: P_b is given in %, raw_value "0", value "0", unit "%"? Actually schema says for bound_probability and unbound_probability, normalize value to unitless 0-1 fraction, unit empty. So P_b raw_value "0", value "0", unit "" (since 0%). But note the paper gives P_b as a percentage; we need to convert to fraction. For HVS1, P_b = 0, so value 0.0. For unbound_probability, the paper doesn't provide that directly; we can compute as 1 - P_b, but schema wants raw_value; raw_value not given. So we'll leave null. But maybe we can set bound_probability only. bound_status_metric: maybe P_b itself is a metric; but we'll leave null. So bound_assessment: escape_velocity null, escape_velocity_ratio null, escape_margin null, bound_probability filled, unbound_probability null.

For photometry, spectroscopy, stellar_parameters, abundances, quality_flags, orbit, astrophysical_origin: we need to extract what's available.

Stellar parameters: Teff, log_g, mass, age, radius, luminosity from stellar_params table. We have those. Also spectral type? Not given explicitly. For HVS1, Teff = 10290 (from atmospheric_params_1.tex), log_g = 3.49, mass = 3.59, age = 247 Myr, radius = 5.6 R_sun, luminosity log(L/L_sun) = 2.50. We'll fill those.

Photometry: Not given in tables; they mention photometry from various surveys but not tabulated. So empty.

Spectroscopy: The atmospheric_params_1 gives radial velocity, v_sini, but those are already in observed_phase_space and stellar_parameters. We can add spectroscopy for v_sini maybe. But radial velocity is in observed_phase_space, so not duplicate. The spectroscopy record can include measurement_type "radial_velocity_follow_up" maybe? The radial velocity from spectroscopic analysis is already in observed_phase_space. However, the schema has spectroscopy with measurement_type "radial_velocity_follow_up" or "line_measurement". The radial velocity from the atmospheric table is the same as we used for observed_phase_space radial_velocity. So we could omit spectroscopy to avoid duplication, but the skill says to fill photometry, spectroscopy, stellar_parameters, etc. We can include spectroscopy with the radial velocity if it's a different measurement (it is the same). But it's okay to have it in observed_phase_space. Better to keep only one radial_velocity entry. So spectroscopy array can be empty or include maybe spectral_type? Not given. So empty.

Quality flags: RUWE < 1.4 for all objects, so we can add a quality flag "RUWE" with value "<1.4" but the paper says all objects have RUWE below 1.4, so we can add generic flag. However, no per-star numeric value. So maybe skip.

Orbit: From kinematic tables, we have plane-crossing quantities: x_p, y_p, z_p, r_p, v_xp, v_yp, v_zp, v_Grf,p, v_ej,p, tau_flight,p. Those are orbit parameters. We could fill pericenter, apocenter, etc., but not directly. Flight time is tau_flight,p. Pericenter maybe r_p is the pericenter distance? Actually r_p is the distance of plane crossing point from the Galactic center, not necessarily pericenter. So we can use "other" named quantities. Better to fill orbit fields with available data: flight_time from tau_flight,p. eccentricity not given. pericenter/apocenter not given. But we can fill zmax? Not given. So we'll use flight_time. In orbit, there is flight_time quantity. We'll set flight_time from tau_flight,p. Example: HVS1 tau_flight,p=176 Myr. So we'll fill orbit.flight_time.

Astrophysical origin: The paper gives origin assessment categories and ejection velocities, and some hypothesis metrics (bound probability). For HVS1, origin not well constrained (unconstrained group). They provide plane-crossing positions and ejection velocity v_ej,p. So origin_site maybe null, origin_classification maybe "unconstrained"? We can fill astrophysical_origin.origin_site: null, origin_classification: QuantityRecord with value? The paper says "The origin of 19 stars in the sample is not really constrained ... All of them could possibly stem from the Galactic disk, and in particular from the GC." So classification is unconstrained. Could put a text value. But quant records need raw_value. We could use the category text from paper: "unconstrained origin". We'll add an ExtraQuantityRecord maybe later. So astrophysical_origin fields: origin_site null, origin_classification maybe a quantity with raw_value "unconstrained" (though it's text, but that's okay for quantity record if value is string). Use origin_classification as QuantityRecord with raw_value and value "unconstrained". But the schema for origin_classification is QuantityRecord, which requires raw_value and value. So we'll set raw_value "unconstrained", value "unconstrained". That seems plausible. ejection_velocity: v_ej,p given. travel_time: tau_flight,p. hypothesis_metrics: maybe bound probability P_b is a metric for Milky Way bound, but that's already in bound_assessment. Not duplicate. We can include P_b as a hypothesis_metric for "origin at GC"? No. So skip.

Now we must fill extra if any.

For each candidate, we need method_refs for each quantity. For distance: step-06 (distance_estimation). For radial_velocity: step-04 (stellar_parameter_inference) since it's from spectral fitting. For proper motions: not available. For parallax: not available. For stellar parameters (Teff, log_g, mass, etc.): step-04. For derived kinematics (galactocentric positions, velocities): step-08 (velocity_calculation) and possibly solar position step-07. We'll use step-08. For bound_assessment: step-10 (escape_or_bound_assessment). For orbit flight_time: step-11 (orbit_integration). For astrophysical_origin ejection_velocity and travel_time: step-11 or step-12? Ejection velocity is from orbit integration and origin assessment, so step-11. For origin_classification: step-12.

We need to include source_refs for each value from the ECSV cells. We'll use the ECSV paths for the relevant tables. For stellar parameters, use table_stellar_params.ecsv. For radial_velocity, use table_tab_atmos_results.ecsv. For kinematic quantities, use table_tab_kinematic_AS.ecsv.

Now for each candidate, we'll construct the object. We'll do them sequentially.

Let's start with HVS1.

Identifiers: record_id "2003.12766:cand-001", paper_candidate_id "HVS1", gaia_source_id "", all as per stub.

Inclusion assessment:
- summary: "HVS1 is one of the 19 HVS stars from the MMT sample reanalyzed. Its bound probability is 0% in both Galactic potential models, indicating it is unbound."
- paper_labels: ["hvs_candidate", "unbound_star"]
- galactic_bound_claim: "unbound"
- inclusion_basis: "explicit_candidate_text"
- extraction_confidence: "high"
- confidence_reason: "The paper explicitly includes HVS1 in the kinematic sample, provides detailed bound assessment, and identifies it as likely unbound."
- source_refs: need text refs from aa.tex that mention HVS1 as candidate. The kinematic tables list HVS1, and its bound probability is 0. Also the text discusses unconstrained group. We'll cite lines where the kinematic table appears and where bound probabilities are discussed. Maybe use the caption of Model I table: line 486-490 shows caption, and the table entry shows P_b=0. So text ref: line 486-490 (caption includes "Kinematic quantities for the program stars based on Model I.") and we can also note the unconstrained group discussion, but not required. So source_refs: [ { kind: "text", path: "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex", start_line: 486, end_line: 490, context: "Kinematic quantities for the program stars based on Model I." }, maybe also line 333-334 about unconstrained group. But enough.

Candidate origin:
- origin_type: "cited_from_literature"
- paper_reassesses_unbound_status: true
- source_refs: text where it cites the MMT survey as source, and where it reanalyzes kinematics. We'll use line 459 (sample of HVSs of Brown+2014) and maybe line 97 (extend analysis). So source_refs: [{ kind: "text", path: "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex", start_line: 459, end_line: 459, context: "the sample of HVSs of \citet{2014ApJ...787...89B}" }]
- citation: {
    bibkey: "2014ApJ...787...89B",
    authors: ["Brown, W. R.", "Geller, M. J.", "Kenyon, S. J."],
    year: "2014",
    title: "",
    doi: "",
    bibcode: "2014ApJ...787...89B",
    arxiv_id: "",
    citation_context_refs: [ ... same as source_refs maybe? Actually citation_context_refs is where the citation appears in the paper. So we can use the same line 459. ],
    bibliography_refs: [ { kind: "text", path: "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.bbl", start_line: 80, end_line: 83, context: "bibitem 2014ApJ...787...89B" } ]
  }

Core:
observed_phase_space: {
  ra: null,
  dec: null,
  distance: { raw_value: "161^{+13}_{-13}", value: "161", error: "", lower_error: "13", upper_error: "13", unit: "kpc", source_refs: [ecsv cell from stellar_params], method_refs: ["step-06"] },
  parallax: null,
  proper_motion_ra: null,
  proper_motion_dec: null,
  radial_velocity: { raw_value: "832.5", value: "832.5", unit: "km s-1", source_refs: [ecsv cell from atmos table], method_refs: ["step-04"] }
}
Note: The radial velocity raw_value in ECSV for HVS1 line 15 col_004 is "832.5". We'll use that. The ECSV path: "literature/2003.12766/catalog_tables/table-tab-atmos-results.ecsv", line 15, column col_004, column_header: "\\varv_rad | (km s -1 )", raw_value "832.5". Good.

For distance: table_stellar_params.ecsv line 39, column col_024 (d | (kpc)), raw_value "161^+13_-13" (note: the cell is "161 ^+13_-13" but raw_value as string "161 ^+13_-13"? The ECSV shows "161 ^+13_-13". We'll use that as raw_value, and value "161", error "", lower_error "13", upper_error "13", unit "kpc". The error fields can be "13" as numbers but should be strings? Schema accepts string. So lower_error: "13". Good.

Derived kinematics: from AS.ecsv line 32-33 for HVS1. x: col_002 raw_value "-101.4", value "-101.4", unit "kpc", source_refs ecsv cell. y: col_003 "-100.9". z: col_004 "83.5". vx: col_006 "-390", vy: "-340", vz: "450". total_velocity: col_009 "710". All method_refs ["step-08"]. Note that v_Grf is total_velocity. We'll fill all those, but note galactocentric_radius is not directly given, we could compute from x,y,z but not required; we can leave null. The schema allows galactocentric_radius optionally. So fill only those present.

Bound_assessment: bound_probability from AS.ecsv line 32 col_011 "P_b | (%)", raw_value "0", value "0" (unitless fraction). method_refs ["step-10"]. Other null.

Stellar_parameters: from stellar_params.ecsv line 39:
mass: col_003 raw_value "3.59 ^+0.06_-0.08", value "3.59", error "", lower_error "0.06", upper_error "0.08", unit "M_sun".
age (tau): col_006 raw_value "247 ^+10_-10", value "247", lower_error "10", upper_error "10", unit "Myr".
radius: col_015 raw_value "5.6 ^+0.4_-0.4", value "5.6", lower_error "0.4", upper_error "0.4", unit "R_sun".
luminosity: col_018 raw_value "2.50 ^+0.05_-0.06", value "2.50", lower_error "0.05", upper_error "0.06", unit "" (since log(L/L_sun) no unit).
teff: from atmos_params table: HVS1 Teff raw_value "10 290", value "10290", unit "K". Also log_g: raw_value "3.49", value "3.49", unit "cgs". We'll use atmos_params.ecsv line 15, col_002 and col_003.

So stellar_parameters: teff, log_g, mass, age, radius, luminosity.

Photometry: empty array.
Spectroscopy: could add v_sini from atmos_params: raw_value "236", value "236", unit "km s-1". That's rotation. We'll include in spectroscopy maybe with measurement_type "line_measurement"? Actually it's projected rotational velocity. spectroscopy record can have measurement_type "line_measurement" or "other". We'll use "line_measurement" and spectral_type "".

Abundances: empty.
Quality_flags: maybe empty.

Orbit: flight_time from AS.ecsv line 32 col_022: tau_flight,p raw_value "176", value "176", unit "Myr". method_refs ["step-11"]. Also could add ejection_velocity in astrophysical_origin, not orbit.

Astrophysical_origin: ejection_velocity: AS.ecsv col_021 v_ej,p raw_value "780", value "780", unit "km s-1". travel_time: same as tau_flight,p but we can put here as well. We'll fill ejection_velocity and travel_time. origin_classification: the paper's category "unconstrained". We'll set origin_classification QuantityRecord with raw_value "unconstrained origin", value "unconstrained". origin_site null. hypothesis_metrics empty.

Extra: none.

Now we need to include source_refs for each cell exactly. We'll create the JSON accordingly.

We'll do this for each candidate 1-8. They are: cand-001 HVS1, cand-002 HVS4, cand-003 HVS5, cand-004 HVS6, cand-005 HVS7, cand-006 HVS8, cand-007 HVS9, cand-008 HVS10.

We need to locate the rows for each in the ECSV files.

Table of rows:
- atmos_results.ecsv: HVS1 line 15 (col_001 "HVS1"), HVS4 line 18-19? Actually line 18 "HVS4", line 19 start of row with Stat. but data row is line 18. The star row is the one with just "HVS4" then values. So HVS4 line 18: "HVS4" "13 890" ... So line 18. HVS5 line 21 "HVS5", line 22-23 Stat/Sys. So line 21. HVS6 line 24 "HVS6", line 25-26. So line 24. HVS7 line 27 "HVS7", line 28-29. line 27. HVS8 line 30 "HVS8". So HVS1:15, HVS4:18, HVS5:21, HVS6:24, HVS7:27, HVS8:30, HVS9:33, HVS10:36.

- stellar_params.ecsv: HVS1 line 39, HVS4 line 40, HVS5 line 41, HVS6 line 42, HVS7 line 43, HVS8 line 44, HVS9 line 45, HVS10 line 46.

- kinematic_AS.ecsv: HVS1 line 32-33 (row with values and Stat line). We need to pick the value row, not the Stat row. So value row line 32, Stat line 33. So we'll use line 32 for values. Similarly for others. HVS4 line 34, HVS5 line 36, HVS6 line 40, HVS7 line 42, HVS8 line 44, HVS9 line 46, HVS10 line 48.

Thus we can extract.

Now, we must ensure that for each quantity, method_refs are exactly one direct step ID. For distance: step-06. For radial_velocity: step-04. For Teff, log_g: step-04. For mass, age, radius, luminosity: derived from step-04 and step-05? Actually those are from evolutionary tracks and photometry? The paper says mass, age, radius derived from position in Kiel diagram compared to evolutionary tracks, using Teff and log_g from spectral analysis. So they are from stellar_parameter_inference (step-04) and maybe also from stellar tracks, but the paper's step-04 covers "stellar parameter inference". So we'll use method_refs ["step-04"] for mass, age, radius, luminosity. The luminosity comes from stellar radius and Teff? That's also from step-04. So fine.

For derived kinematics: step-08 (velocity_calculation). For total_velocity (v_Grf) also step-08. For bound_probability: step-10. For flight_time: step-11. For ejection_velocity: from orbit integration step-11, but also could be step-12? The paper computes ejection velocity from plane crossing with correction for Galactic rotation. That's part of origin assessment? Actually the paper describes it in section 5.3 "Ejection velocities" as derived from orbit integration. So step-11 (orbit_integration) produces plane crossing parameters including ejection velocity. So method_refs ["step-11"]. For origin_classification: step-12.

Now we'll construct the JSON for each candidate systematically.

Let's start writing the JSON output.
